note: Pre-v2-§23 dialogue turns for mavros — generated with the pre-§23 _SYSTEM_PROMPT (no anti-anthropomorphisation guards). Captured 2026-05-24 before the dialogue state is reverted for the §23 verification walkthrough. Kept as the baseline-before-the-fix research record.

dialogue_turns.content:
Looking back across your modules, your reflections show some shifts in where your attention landed. In earlier modules, you often considered the mechanics of large language models and their general reliability. Later on, your reflections began to focus more on things like pedagogical fit, auditing tools, and the impact on learning. Teacher oversight remained a consistent area of reflection throughout.

What do you notice about how your attention moved across these modules?
It feels like the early focus on tools was necessary scaffolding before I could ask the harder pedagogical questions. The order makes sense in retrospect, even though I didn't plan it.

You described an early focus on tools as "necessary scaffolding" before you could ask "harder pedagogical questions," and that the order now "makes sense."

Can you recall a specific moment or module where you felt that scaffolding was sufficiently in place, allowing you to begin asking those harder questions?

Two themes from your reflections sit beside each other here. In your early modules, your writing returned to the theme of 'LLM mechanics'. Then, in later modules, the theme of 'auditing tools' became more present in your reflections.

What do you make of these two themes being present in your reflective journey?
I don't see it as a contradiction either. Understanding the tool was a precondition for asking whether it fit my teaching. It is the same line of inquiry getting more specific.

You said that understanding the tool was a precondition for asking about pedagogical fit, and that this represents the same line of inquiry getting more specific.

How did your reflections on "LLM mechanics" specifically inform your later explorations of "auditing tools"?

You've spoken about how understanding the tools was a necessary step before you could delve into pedagogical questions, seeing it as a deepening line of inquiry. Considering this journey, what is one concrete, near-term action you will take in your classroom, perhaps next week, that reflects this evolving understanding of AI and its fit with your teaching?
I will try the 'rewrite in your own words' step in my Wednesday class with the older group. Probably at the second lesson of the week — they are usually more engaged then.

You've identified a specific group and timing for trying the 'rewrite in your own words' step. When you introduce this to them, what will you tell the students about why they are doing it, and what will you be listening or looking for to understand how it's going?

Across your fifteen modules, your attention travelled. In the early modules, your reflections often returned to the mechanics of Large Language Models and the general reliability of the tools. You noted that this early focus on tools felt like "necessary scaffolding" before you could ask harder questions. This attention to how the tools worked was a precondition for asking whether they fit your teaching, which you described as "the same line of inquiry getting more specific."

As you moved through the modules, your focus shifted. While "teacher oversight" remained a consistent theme in your reflections from beginning to end, the prominence of LLM mechanics and general reliability faded. Instead, your reflections began to cover "pedagogical fit," "auditing tools," and "learning impact" more extensively. You reflected that the order of the modules made sense in retrospect, even though you hadn't planned it this way. You engaged with 22 distinct professional tensions across the programme, exploring the nuances of these shifts in your thinking.

As you look forward, you articulated a concrete step you plan to take. You will try the "rewrite in your own words" step in your Wednesday class with the older group, specifically at the second lesson of the week, as you noted "they are usually more engaged then."

Across your fifteen modules, your attention travelled, shifting and deepening along a consistent line of inquiry. In the early modules, your reflections often returned to the mechanics of Large Language Models and general questions of reliability. This focus on how the tools worked felt, as you described it, like "necessary scaffolding" for the deeper questions that emerged later.

As you moved through the modules, your reflections began to embrace new themes. By the sixth module, you were writing more extensively about pedagogical fit, the impact on learning, and the practicalities of auditing tools. This wasn't a departure from your earlier concerns, but rather, as you put it, "the same line of inquiry getting more specific." You noted that understanding the tool was a "precondition for asking whether it fit my teaching." The consistent thread throughout this journey was your attention to teacher oversight, a theme that remained present in your reflections from beginning to end.

You engaged with twenty-two distinct professional tensions, navigating the complexities of integrating AI into your practice. Your journey illustrates how an initial focus on the technical aspects of AI can evolve into a rich exploration of its educational implications. As you look forward, you articulated a clear, concrete step: "I will try the 'rewrite in your own words' step in my Wednesday class with the older group. Probably at the second lesson of the week – they are usually more engaged then." This commitment reflects the practical, pedagogical lens you now bring to AI in education.

Across your fifteen modules, your attention travelled, weaving through various professional tensions and commitments. In the early modules, your reflections often explored the inner workings of large language models, focusing on their mechanics and general reliability. This initial engagement with how the tools functioned felt, in your own words, like "necessary scaffolding before I could ask the harder pedagogical questions." You noted that the order of modules "makes sense in retrospect, even though I didn't plan it."

As you moved through the programme, your focus shifted. While teacher oversight remained a consistent area of reflection throughout, themes like auditing tools, learning impact, and pedagogical fit began to emerge more prominently in your writing from the middle modules onward. The earlier emphasis on LLM mechanics and general reliability gradually received less attention. When reflecting on the presence of both "LLM mechanics" in early modules and "auditing tools" later, you saw no contradiction, describing it as "the same line of inquiry getting more specific." For you, "understanding the tool was a precondition for asking whether it fit my teaching."

You engaged with 22 distinct professional tensions throughout your journey. As you look forward, you articulated a clear, concrete step: "I will try the 'rewrite in your own words' step in my Wednesday class with the older group. Probably at the second lesson of the week — they are usually more engaged then."
